coredata.dc:title: Flexible and foldable films of wCDs/BNNS-OH/PVA white light composite with high stability and heat dissipation
coredata.dc:creator: He, Lili
Liu, Rui
Ge, Chunhua
Ma, Chunxue
Yang, Li
Zhang, Xiangdong
coredata.dc:description: Recently, carbon dots (CDs) have gained much attention in optoelectronic fields on account of their superior optical and environmentally friendly properties. Nevertheless, aggregation-induced quenching (AIQ) effect of solid-state CDs and thermal quenching caused by high temperatures severely hinders their further development. Herein, a novel wCDs/BNNS-OH hybrid nanostructure was synthesized by combining blue CDs (bCDs) and yellow CDs (yCDs) to the hydroxylated boron nitride nanosheets (BNNS–OH) surface. The uniform dispersion of CDs in BNNS-OH effectively alleviates the undesirable AIQ effect, and enables CDs/BNNS-OH composites to have color-tunable fluorescence characteristics. The wCDs/BNNS-OH/PVA composite film was obtained by encapsulating wCDs/BNNS-OH into polyvinyl alcohol (PVA). The film exhibits excellent luminescence stability in the air, ultraviolet light, water, and high-temperature environment. Meanwhile, since wCDs/BNNS-OH establishes an effective heat transfer channel in the PVA matrix, the working temperature of CDs in the wCDs/BNNS-OH/PVA film is greatly reduced. Moreover, the prepared wCDs/BNNS-OH/PVA film is flexible enough to retain the photoluminescence intensity unchanged after thousands of bending cycles. This high stability and heat dissipation wCDs/BNNS-OH/PVA composite film will facilitate the application of CDs in flexible and foldable lighting devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Boron nitride nanosheets
White light composite films
Stability
Heat dissipation
Flexible

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optical performances of wCDs/BNNS-OH/PVA composite films
Heat dissipation of wCDs/BNNS-OH/PVA composite films
Mechanical flexibility of wCDs/BNNS-OH/PVA composite films

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FTIR spectra and (b) XRD patterns of bCD, yCDs, wCDs/BNNS-OH and BNNS-OH samples.
(a, b) SEM images of pure BNNS-OH and wCDs/BNNS-OH. (c, d) TEM images of bCDs and yCDs, respectively. (e, f) TEM and HRTEM images of wCDs/BNNS-OH, respectively.

(a) PL spectra of bCDs/BNNS-OH and yCDs/BNNS-OH. (b) wCDs/BNNS-OH (1–5) with different ratios of bCDs and yCDs (1:1, 1:2, 1:3, 1:4 and 1:5, respectively). (c) Photographs of bCDs/BNNS-OH (no. 1), wCDs/BNNS-OH (1–5) (no. 2–6) and yCDs/BNNS-OH (no. 7) under day-light and UV-light. (d) CIE chromaticity diagram of bCDs/BNNS-OH, yCDs/BNNS-OH and wCDs/BNNS-OH (1–5), respectively. (e) PL spectra of wCDs/BNNS-OH (6–9) with different ratios of CDs and BNNS-OH (2:1, 1:1, 1:1.5 and 1:2, respectively).
PL spectra of wCDs/BNNS-OH/PVA composite film and PVA film. The inset shows photographs of (a) wCDs/BNNS-OH/PVA composite film and (b) PVA film under day-light (left) and UV-light (right).

Stability tests of wCDs/BNNS-OH/PVA composite film under 365 nm excitation wavelength, (a) in air, (b) UV-light radiation, (c) soaked in water (inset: photograph of wCDs/BNNS-OH/PVA film in water), and (d) under diverse temperature surroundings.
Infrared thermal imaging of (a) wCDs/BNNS-OH/PVA composite film and (b) wCDs/PVA film on LED chips. (c) The diagram of heat flow transfer in wCDs/BNNS-OH/PVA composite films.

(a) Foldable, and (b) formability of wCDs/BNNS-OH/PVA composite films (c) PL intensity retention of wCDs/BNNS-OH/PVA film after 1000 bending times (inset: photograph of wCDs/BNNS-OH/PVA film at original and bending state). (d) Tensile curves of wCDs/BNNS-OH/PVA film (inset: photograph of wCDs/BNNS-OH/PVA film at tensile fracture under UV-light).
Schematic illustration for preparation process of (a) wCDs/BNNS-OH composites and (b) wCDs/BNNS-OH/PVA composite films.

head.ce:title: Flexible and foldable films of wCDs/BNNS-OH/PVA white light composite with high stability and heat dissipation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, Chongshan Road No. 66, Shenyang, 110036, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, Chongshan Road No. 66, Shenyang, 110036, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recently, carbon dots (CDs) have gained much attention in optoelectronic fields on account of their superior optical and environmentally friendly properties. Nevertheless, aggregation-induced quenching (AIQ) effect of solid-state CDs and thermal quenching caused by high temperatures severely hinders their further development. Herein, a novel wCDs/BNNS-OH hybrid nanostructure was synthesized by combining blue CDs (bCDs) and yellow CDs (yCDs) to the hydroxylated boron nitride nanosheets (BNNS–OH) surface. The uniform dispersion of CDs in BNNS-OH effectively alleviates the undesirable AIQ effect, and enables CDs/BNNS-OH composites to have color-tunable fluorescence characteristics. The wCDs/BNNS-OH/PVA composite film was obtained by encapsulating wCDs/BNNS-OH into polyvinyl alcohol (PVA). The film exhibits excellent luminescence stability in the air, ultraviolet light, water, and high-temperature environment. Meanwhile, since wCDs/BNNS-OH establishes an effective heat transfer channel in the PVA matrix, the working temperature of CDs in the wCDs/BNNS-OH/PVA film is greatly reduced. Moreover, the prepared wCDs/BNNS-OH/PVA film is flexible enough to retain the photoluminescence intensity unchanged after thousands of bending cycles. This high stability and heat dissipation wCDs/BNNS-OH/PVA composite film will facilitate the application of CDs in flexible and foldable lighting devices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), as carbon-based fluorescent nanoparticles free of heavy metals, have become a perfect substitute for rare-earth phosphors and semiconductor quantum dots due to their simple synthesis method, adjustable luminescence color, low toxicity, and low cost [1–3]. As we all know, CDs will be accompanied by a sharp deterioration of photoluminescence performance when they are dried into powders, which greatly limits their wide application in solid-state lighting and display fields [4–6]. At present, enormous efforts have been invested in combating the aggregation-induced quenching (AIQ) effect of solid-state CDs. Studies have shown that one of the most effective ways to solve this problem is to embed CDs in a suitable solid matrix, including polymers [7,8], organic matrix (e.g. starch) [9], and inorganic matrix (e.g. SiO2, BaSO4) [10,11]. The AIQ problem is alleviated as the CDs are evenly dispersed and protected by the matrix material. Nevertheless, another non-negligible issue for CDs is the structural degradation and surface group changes of CDs at high temperatures, resulting in the reduction of luminescence activity. According to reports, the fluorescent structure of CDs can be easily destroyed even at 80 °C [12]. It has been confirmed that the phosphor layer can easily exceed 130 °C and even 150 °C when the white light-emitting diodes (WLEDs) device is in operation [13–15], which obviously exceeds the heat capacity limit of CDs. Such a high temperature makes the heat dissipation of CDs in high-power lighting devices a huge challenge.

Cooling CD nanoparticles is a difficult task because it not only self-heats on account of Stokes loss, but they are also typically encapsulated in polymers with low thermal conductivity to prevent AIQ effects. It may be a direct and effective pathway to solve this problem by enhancing the thermal conductivity of CDs composites to improve its heat dissipation capacity and reduce its working temperature [16–18]. But so far, thermally conductive materials suitable for enhancing the thermal conductivity of luminescent composites are scarce. As thermally conductive materials are mostly light-absorbing substances, this will inevitably decrease the luminescent properties of optical materials even under low load [19–21]. The ideal reinforcing filler for optical materials of electronic devices must have both high thermal conductivity and high visible light transparency simultaneously. Therefore, there is an urgent need to find a thermal conductive matrix suitable for CDs to promote the heat dissipation of CDs composites without sacrificing their optical properties, which will be of great significance to thermal management of high-performance lighting and display equipment packages.

Two-dimensional (2D) boron nitride nanosheets (BNNS), with good electrical insulation, ultra-high thermal conductivity, outstanding oxidation resistance and, low toxicity, have broad application prospects in semiconductor devices, insulation thermal management, and photoelectric fields [22–25]. Most notably, the visible light absorption of BNNS is almost negligible, which ensures that luminescence of the composite will not be impaired by its existence [26–28]. In particular, functionalized BNNS have surface groups like –OH or –NH2 that provide reaction sites for directly binding various materials in physical or chemical ways [29,30]. When the light-emitting center is in close contact with the BNNS, the excess heat will be exported through the BNNS to avoid high working temperatures caused by serious heat accumulation in the luminescent layer. However, at present, although BNNS has been extensively applied in polymer materials as thermally conductive filler, there are few reports on the research as an inorganic matrix in CDs luminescent materials [31,32].

Nowadays, with the rapid development of wearable electronic technology, the market demand for flexible LED and flexible display is increasing [33–35]. Compared with conventional phosphors, flexible luminescent materials have potential applications value in foldable optoelectronic devices owing to their deformability and crack resistance [36–38]. In order to extend the application of CDs composites to flexible optoelectronic fields, it will definitely be a concern to develop flexible CDs-based light-emitting films with high stability and excellent mechanical deformability.

In this study, a simple, efficient, and low-cost strategy is proposed by simultaneously assembling two kinds of fluorescent CDs onto hydroxyl-modified BNNS (BNNS–OH) surfaces. BNNS-OH is not only the host for inhibiting disagreeable AIQ of CDs but also thermal conductive filler that improves the heat dissipation capacity of CDs-based luminescent films. A series of wCDs/BNNS-OH composites with adjustable luminescent color was obtained via adjusting the ratio of blue-emission CDs (bCDs), yellow-emission CDs (yCDs), and BNNS-OH. The structure, morphology and fluorescence properties of wCDs/BNNS-OH composites were studied in detail. Furthermore, to improve the practicability of CDs-based luminescent materials in flexible displays, the optimum white-light emission wCDs/BNNS-OH was encapsulated into polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) with good film-forming and mechanical properties, and a wCDs/BNNS-OH/PVA flexible luminescent composite film was prepared. The composite film exhibits excellent long-term stability in the air, ultraviolet light, water, and high-temperature environment. More importantly, wCDs/BNNS-OH/PVA composite film displays outstanding heat dissipation capability in the application of LED lamps. Meanwhile, the photoluminescence intensity could still be maintained after thousands of bending and folding cycles. Accordingly, the design of wCDs/BNNS-OH/PVA composite film with high stable luminescence, heat dissipation, and flexibility will promote the rapid development of CDs in the high-power lighting and flexible displays field.

The hexagonal boron nitride (h-BN, ≥99.9%) were provided by Aladdin Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China), potassium hydroxide (KOH, ≥85%), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, ≥96%), o-phenylenediamine (o-PD, C6H8N2, ≥98.5%), citric acid monohydrate (CA, C6H8O7·H2O, ≥99.8%), and polyvinyl alcohol-1799 (PVA, (C2H4O)n, ≥98%) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shenyang, China). All chemicals are analytical reagent grade and used without further purification.

The blue- and yellow-light-emitting CDs (bCDs and yCDs) were synthesized using o-PD as raw materials by adding and not adding citric acid [39,40]. Based on the strategy reported in the literature, BNNS-OH was successfully synthesized [41]. Scheme 1 a exhibits the preparation process of the wCDs/BNNS-OH composites. In a typical process of synthesizing wCDs/BNNS-OH, BNNS-OH (2.0 g) was added to the aqueous solution of CDs (2 mL), then stirred for 4 h at room temperature. Subsequently, naturally dried in air to afford a white emission composite powder, which was named wCDs/BNNS-OH. In order to obtain the wCDs/BNNS-OH composite sample with the optimum white light emission, wCDs/BNNS-OH (1–5) were synthesized by regulating the ratios of bCDs and yCDs in CDs mixed solution to 1:1, 1:2, 1:3, 1:4, and 1:5. When the ratio of bCDs and yCDs was fixed at 1:3, wCDs/BNNS-OH (6–9) were prepared by adjusting the ratios of CDs and BNNS-OH to 2:1, 1:1, 1:1.5, and 1:2. For comparison, bCDs/BNNS-OH and yCDs/BNNS-OH were synthesized in the presence of only one of bCDs or yCDs, respectively, and the synthesis steps were the same as that of wCDs/BNNS-OH.

Scheme 1b presents the preparation process for the wCDs/BNNS-OH/PVA composite films. PVA (0.8 g) was dissolved in deionized water (30 mL) via heating and stirring at 95 °C until completely dissolved, and then wCDs/BNNS-OH (0.2 g) were added. An additional 30 min of water bath ultrasound was used to ensure good dispersion of wCDs/BNNS-OH in the PVA polymer. Finally, the above polymer aqueous solution was uniformly coated on a flat and clean glass mold, and wCDs/BNNS-OH/PVA composite films were peeled off from the mold after natural drying. For comparison, the wCDs/PVA films containing only CDs were fabricated using the same process as described above.

The surface functional groups and crystalline structure were determined using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Nicolet 6700) and X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Bruker D8) with Cu Kα radiation. The micro-morphology was observed using a scanning electron microscope (SEM, JSM-6711F), transmission electron microscopic (TEM), and high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM, JEM-2100F). Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were performed with a fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi F-4500) and the chromaticity coordinates were calculated by the CIE 1931 standard colorimetric system. Thermal image was taken via an infrared (IR) camera (Fotric 220s). Tensile test was measured by a universal tensile machine (Shenzhen SANS test machine UTM 4204).

The chemical feature and crystal structure of wCDs/BNNS-OH samples were clarified by FTIR and XRD tests, demonstrated in Fig. 1 and Fig. S1. As Fig. 1a describes, the broadband of CDs around 3433 cm−1 is ascribed to stretching vibrations of O–H and N–H. The absorption located around 2932 cm−1 derived from the stretching vibration of C–H bond. Meanwhile, the absorption bands about 1627, 1506, 1378, and 1051 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibration of C=N/C=O, C=C, C–N, and C–O–C, respectively. These results indicate that functional groups such as hydroxyl (-OH), carboxyl (-COOH), and amino (-NH2) groups exist on the surface of CDs [42,43]. BNNS-OH has a strong absorption band at 1375 cm−1 and a weak band at 817 cm−1, which are attributed to B–N stretching vibration and B–N–B bending vibration, respectively. Besides, the absorption band centered at 3433 cm−1 corresponds to O–H stretching vibration. This phenomenon demonstrates that abundant –OH groups have been successfully grafted onto BNNS-OH [44]. Therefore, during the preparation of wCDs/BNNS-OH composites, hydrogen bonds will be formed between –OH/-COOH/-NH2 of CDs and –OH of BNNS-OH. Naturally, the characteristic absorption bands of CDs and BNNS-OH were observed simultaneously in the FTIR spectra of wCDs/BNNS-OH.

Fig. 1b displays XRD pattern of sample powder. The CDs have an obvious broad peak at the center of about 2θ = 22.1°, which is characteristic of amorphous carbon [45]. The main diffraction peaks of BNNS-OH at 2θ = 26.6°, 41.5°, 50.1°, and 55.2° are attributed to (002), (100), (102) and (104) crystal planes, respectively, which perfectly match the standard card (PDF#34–0421) of h-BN phase. After loading CDs, obvious BNNS-OH characteristic diffraction peak and weak CDs characteristic peak were detected in wCDs/BNNS-OH. It suggests that the basic structure of wCDs/BNNS-OH is still h-BN, and the crystal phase and orientation have not obviously changed after the CD modification. It may be related to the weak diffraction peaks of CDs itself and the low content of CDs in wCDs/BNNS-OH composites. Also, the above analysis results provide a basis for the successful preparation of two CDs and BNNS-OH co-assembled composites.

The micro-morphology of wCDs/BNNS-OH composites was analyzed by SEM, TEM, and HRTEM. The SEM images of pure BNNS-OH are thin sheet structures with different lateral dimensions, and the surface is smooth (Fig. 2 a). The addition of CDs will not significantly change the original morphology of BNNS-OH, but the surface of wCDs/BNNS-OH will obviously become rough (Fig. 2b). Through TEM observation, it can be seen that bCDs and yCDs are uniform spherical particles (Fig. 2c and d). The TEM image of wCDs/BNNS-OH showed that the dot-like CDs adhered to the surface of BNNS-OH uniformly and without obvious aggregation (Fig. 2e and f). The lattice fringes of CDs in the HRTEM image are clearly visible. The lattice spacing is 0.21 nm, corresponding to [100] crystal plane of graphene [46], which further reveals the existence of CDs.

In addition, EDS-Mapping analysis was performed on the wCDs/BNNS-OH composites (Fig. S2). The results show that the wCDs/BNNS-OH is composed of four elements, including B, N, C, and O. The mapping image shows that each element is evenly distributed in wCDs/BNNS-OH composites. In fact, these mainly benefit from typical 2D structure of BNNS-OH, which have a relatively high specific surface area and provide sufficient growth sites for CDs [47].

In the formation of wCDs/BNNS-OH composites, hydrogen bonds are considered to be the driving force of CDs adsorption on BNNS, and the ratio of bCDs, yCDs and BNNS-OH is an important parameter to control the luminescence color and intensity of wCDs/BNNS-OH composites. Fig. 3 a and Fig. 3b show the PL spectra of the synthesized bCDs/BNNS-OH, yCDs/BNNS-OH and wCDs/BNNS-OH (1–5) composites. Under 365 nm excitation wavelength, two emission peaks near 448 nm and 550 nm can be observed, corresponding to the characteristic emission peaks of bCDs and yCDs, respectively (Fig. S3). It is worth noting that with the increasing number of yCDs, the intensity of the characteristic peak at about 448 nm decreases monotonically, while that at 550 nm increases gradually. The mechanism of this phenomenon might be ascribed to the Förster distance resonance energy transfer (FRET) between bCDs and yCDs [48,49]. Besides, the PL spectra of wCDs/BNNS-OH seems to show extra emission from 460 nm to 500 nm. In fact, it has been reported in the literature that CDs in carbon-based films, aerogels, and MOF composites exhibit a certain degree of change in PL emission [50–52]. At present, the PL mechanism of CDs is still controversial, surface state emission and eigenstate emission are generally accepted, and the synergy between them affects the PL of CDs [53–55]. In addition, as the environment around the CD changes, the emission changes accordingly. Therefore, the formation of extra emission may be the result of the combined effect of bCDs, yCDs, and the BNNS-OH matrix.

Color changes of the prepared composite samples are shown in Fig. 3c. The corresponding CIE chromaticity coordinates were calculated by PL emission data, as shown in Fig. 3d. The bCDs/BNNS-OH has blue light emission and CIE coordinates are (0.1613, 0.1550). The yCDs/BNNS-OH has yellow light emission and CIE coordinates are (0.4582, 0.5181). The CIE coordinates of wCDs/BNNS-OH (1–5) composites are (0.2597, 0.2851), (0.2843, 0.3242), (0.3079, 0.3557), (0.3314, 0.3834), (0.3457, 0.4135), which were consistent with color changes of corresponding sample. The above results prove that the color of wCDs/BNNS-OH composites can be adjusted by controlling the ratio of bCDs and yCDs.

To optimize the luminescence intensity of wCDs/BNNS-OH composites, the ratio of bCDs to yCDs was fixed at 1:3, and influence of the ratio of CDs to BNNS-OH on wCDs/BNNS-OH composites fluorescence properties was further studied. Fig. 3e shows the PL emission spectra of wCDs/BNNS-OH (6–9) composites synthesized with different amounts of BNNS-OH. It can be seen that the number of BNNS-OH added has little effect on the emission peak position. At the same time, with the increase of BNNS-OH content in wCDs/BNNS-OH composites, the corresponding emission peak intensity increases sharply at first, reaching the maximum when the ratio of CDs and BNNS-OH is 1:1, and then decreases slightly. The possible reason is that a small amount of BNNS-OH cannot provide enough growth sites for CDs, causing the aggregation of CDs on the surface of BNNS-OH, while excessive BNNS-OH leads to insufficient growth of CDs [56,57]. Therefore, on the basis of the above experiments, the optimal white-light wCDs/BNNS-OH composites used in the subsequent research of this work were prepared with the ratios of 1:3 for bCDs and yCDs, and the ratios of 1:1 for CDs and BNNS-OH, unless otherwise specified.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical performances of wCDs/BNNS-OH/PVA composite films
ce:section.ce:para: To extend the application of wCDs/BNNS-OH composites to the field of flexible optoelectronics, wCDs/BNNS-OH/PVA fluorescent composite films were prepared by mixing wCDs/BNNS-OH and PVA in distilled water and then drying naturally in air, and detailed preparation method is illustrated in experimental part. Fig. 4 shows PL spectra of individual PVA film and wCDs/BNNS-OH/PVA composite film, respectively. When wCDs/BNNS-OH powder is mixed with PVA, the characteristic emission peak of yCDs in wCDs/BNNS-OH/PVA composite film shows a blue shift, which is consistent with the results reported in the literature [58]. As revealed in illustrated photographs of Fig. 4, the original PVA film is colorless and transparent, with almost no PL emission under UV-light. Notably, wCDs/BNNS-OH/PVA composite film can be observed bright white light under UV-light, and the CIE coordinate is (0.2692, 0.3340) (Fig. S4). This indicates that wCDs/BNNS-OH is successfully encapsulated in PVA polymer materials.

To evaluate the usability of wCDs/BNNS-OH/PVA composite film in WLEDs and display device fields, the stability of wCDs/BNNS-OH/PVA composite films in the air, UV-light, water, and the high temperature was studied. The PL intensity of wCDs/BNNS-OH/PVA composite films can be maintained above 90% when exposed to the surrounding environment for 35 days (Fig. 5 a). After 96 h of continuous exposure to UV-light, the film retains 80% of the original PL intensity (Fig. 5b). However, under the same environmental conditions, the PL strength of wCDs/BNNS-OH composite powders decreased significantly. This indicates that under the protection of PVA polymer material, wCDs/BNNS-OH can effectively prevent direct contact with air and slow down the influence of UV-light radiation so that wCDs/BNNS-OH/PVA composite films still have high luminescence stability when exposed to the external environment for a long time. Meanwhile, wCDs/BNNS-OH/PVA composite films also showed excellent water resistance (Fig. 5c), retaining their initial white light emission and about 80% PL strength after 35 days of immersion in water. The good water resistance maybe profit from PVA film formation and the hydrogen bonding among CDs, BNNS-OH, and PVA [59]. BNNS-OH and PVA can simultaneously constrain CDs and prevent them from dissociating into the water, thereby preserving the original luminescence of CDs in wCDs/BNNS-OH/PVA composite films. Besides, after heating the samples from 25 °C to 150 °C for 6 h, wCDs/BNNS-OH/PVA composite films and wCDs/BNNS-OH composite powder showed stable luminescence properties below 100 °C. In contrast, the strength of wCDs/PVA composite film decreases significantly with increasing heating temperature (Fig. 5d), which further demonstrates the importance of BNNS presence for thermal stability of wCDs/BNNS-OH/PVA composite films. The BNNS is an excellent thermal conductivity material, which is beneficial to alleviate the thermal stress inside wCDs/BNNS-OH/PVA composite film, thereby mitigating degradation and aggregation of CDs under thermal stress [60].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Heat dissipation of wCDs/BNNS-OH/PVA composite films
ce:section.ce:para: In view of the promising thermal conductivity of 2D BNNS, the heat dissipation capacity of wCDs/BNNS-OH/PVA composite films have also been explored. As revealed in Fig. 6 a and Fig. 6b, the wCDs/BNNS-OH/PVA composite film was firmly attached to the untreated commercial LED chip, while the wCDs/PVA film was constructed for comparison. After turning on the device, the infrared thermal images show that the operating temperature of the LED chip presents an obvious upward trend. However, the LED surface temperatures using wCDs/BNNS-OH/PVA are much lower than that of wCDs/PVA, with a maximum temperature difference of 26.7 °C when running for 180 s. This may be that wCDs/BNNS establishes an effective heat transfer channel in the PVA matrix, enabling the heat generated to effectively disperse into the surrounding environment, demonstrated in Fig. 6c. It should be noted that the service life of LED lamp is closely related to the working temperature, and the lifespan may increase by 50% for every 10 °C drop in temperature [61]. As a result, the wCDs/BNNS-OH/PVA composite film will have favorable adaptability in high-temperature working situations, and its outstanding heat dissipation capacity can improve the working efficiency and prolong the service life of electronic devices.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanical flexibility of wCDs/BNNS-OH/PVA composite films
ce:section.ce:para: In addition to excellent optical stability and thermal dispersion, the superior luminescence performances of films under deformation are also important in practical applications. As shown in Fig. 7 a and b, the wCDs/BNNS-OH/PVA composite film has sufficient flexibility and will not damage its luminescence during complex folding and arbitrary cutting into various shapes (e.g. “the paper crane” and “LNU”). More surprisingly, almost no decrease in PL strength of the film was observed after bending cycles (Fig. 7c). Furthermore, the wCDs/BNNS-OH/PVA composite film shows a tensile strength of 52 MPa (Fig. 7d). Even after being stretched and broken, it still glows bright white light under UV-light. All these results indicate wCDs/BNNS-OH/PVA has great mechanical flexibility and stable light emission when folded, bent, and stretched deformation, which is expected to be used in flexible LED and flexible display applications.

In summary, a novel wCDs/BNNS-OH hybrid nanostructure with adjustable luminescence color was synthesized through hydrogen bond interaction. Further, wCDs/BNNS-OH with the optimal white light emission was encapsulated in PVA, and the wCDs/BNNS-OH/PVA flexible composite film was obtained. Thanks to the effective protection of the PVA matrix to wCDs/BNNS-OH, wCDs/BNNS-OH/PVA composite film has good luminescence stability in the air, UV-light, water, and high-temperature environment. More importantly, owing to wCDs/BNNS-OH establishes an effective heat transfer channel in the PVA matrix, greatly reducing the operating temperature of CDs when wCDs/BNNS-OH/PVA film is used in light-emitting devices. Furthermore, wCDs/BNNS-OH/PVA films are sufficiently flexible and foldable to be repeatedly bent and keep the PL strength basically unchanged. The findings of this study offer some inspirations to solving the aggregation-induced quenching and thermal quenching of solid CDs simultaneously, and open a door gateway for developing solid-state CDs composites toward emerging flexible and foldable lighting technology.

Lili He: Formal analysis, Data curation, Methodology, Software, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Rui Liu: Formal analysis, Data curation, Validation, Visualization, Software. Chunhua Ge: Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Chunxue Ma: Formal analysis, Data curation, Validation, Visualization. Li Yang: Formal analysis, Data curation, Validation. Xiangdong Zhang: Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The present project was supported by Natural Science Foundation of the Education Department of Liaoning Provincial (LJC201916 and LFW201901).

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2022.110736.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The synthetic strategies, photoluminescence mechanisms and promising applications of carbon dots: current state and future perspective

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. He, P. Xu, X. Zhang, W. Long, The synthetic strategies, photoluminescence mechanisms and promising applications of carbon dots: Current state and future perspective. Carbon 186 (2022) 91-127.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The development of carbon dots: from the perspective of materials chemistry

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Li, L. Li, H. Tu, H. Zhang, D. S. Silvester, C. E. Banks, G. Zou, H. Hou, X. Ji, The development of carbon dots: From the perspective of materials chemistry. Materials Today 51 (2021) 188-207.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile microwave synthesis of carbon dots powder with enhanced solid-state fluorescence and its applications in rapid fingerprints detection and white-light-emitting diodes
